ef7f6b0ac2787fcd.subject: Christopher Caldwell: Inside Donald Trump’s Ruthless Return
ef7f6b0ac2787fcd.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.thefp.com/cp/206347818

Three weeks ago, conventional wisdom held that readers were bored by books about Donald Trump. What more was there to say about the man who has dominated American public life for a decade? Then Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan published Regime Change.
Written by two reporters with unrivaled access to the White House, the book sold more than 300,000 copies in its first week—making it the fastest-selling nonfiction hardcover of the year so far. It argues that Trump’s return to office marks a fundamental transformation of the presidency, defined by an unprecedented expansion of executive power. Is that a convincing argument? We asked the conservative writer Christopher Caldwell to read the book and let us know what he thought. One of the sharpest observers of the rise of Trump, and the big-picture trends that brought him to power, Caldwell offers his view not just of Regime Change but of the meaning of Trump’s second term in today’s Big Read. —The Editors
“Essentially I won every fucking time,” Donald Trump told Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan in March, in a profanity-laced valedictory interview for their new book, Regime Change [ https://substack.com/redirect/f62b0479-1c0f-4edb-8ff2-a868c355b81f?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]. “And I’m tired of winning and winning and winning and just getting bad fucking press.”
What citizen of any nation would wish such a vulgarian at its head? And yet who can doubt that he’s right, at least sometimes? This is the paradox: Understanding the crudest man ever to occupy the White House requires delicacy. If deep access to White House secrets has made this book a bestseller since its June release, what has turned it into a bestseller of warehouse-emptying proportions is its nuance.
Nuance has lately been in short supply. The president has suffered a humiliating defeat in a Middle Eastern war he launched against the wiser counsel of most of his advisers. The middle class is discontented, and the clamor for economic redistribution is louder than it has been since the 1930s. Many of Trump’s detractors claim to have told us so. Elect a barbarian and you’ll get a barbarian. Simple as that. What did you expect?...

aa779d8a6bca12d5.sender: Today's Global Threads from Peter Frankopan <peterfrankopan@substack.com>
aa779d8a6bca12d5.subject: Why Brazil Lost the World Cup 500 Years Ago
aa779d8a6bca12d5.body:
View this post on the web at https://peterfrankopan.substack.com/p/why-brazil-lost-the-world-cup-500

One of the more unexpected explanations for Brazil’s World Cup exit has had nothing to do with tactics, pressing systems or penalty shoot-outs. 
Instead, the five time champions crashed out of the 2026 World Cup because of... Martin Luther.
Global Threads with Peter Frankopan is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
Spend any time reading Brazilian social media over the last few days and you’ll find a surprising number of posts arguing that Brazil has abandoned something essential about itself: football has become too serious, too disciplined, too joyless. 
The culprit, according to many chatting online, is not the manager or the federation but the rapid growth of evangelical Protestantism, whose values are said to have displaced the carefree, exuberant footballing culture that once defined Brazil.
Here’s how the Institute for Hispano Studies put it.
(As a ‘think tank for the study of Hispanic-Catholic Civilization & Pan-Iberian geopolitics’, the Institute has a horse in this race, so some generous pinches of salt are worth sprinkling liberally).
It certainly sounds plausible: Brazil’s greatest teams seemed to play with smiles on their faces. 
There was Pelé, who often spoke about football as happiness. Or Garrincha treated defenders - and sometimes life itself - as a joke. 
Or Ronaldinho grinned through matches that looked less like sporting contests than improvised street performances. 
Careca, Falcao, Rivaldo, Ronaldo, Eder, Jairzinho - they all came across as players who would be as dangerous off the pitch as on it; and who might need a quick visit to the confessional on a regular basis.
Even Sócrates, physician, intellectual and political activist, embodied a football culture that prized imagination over instruction. 
Through the golden years, Brazil teams were synonymous with joy, with parties, with  music, with samba. There was an unmistakable sense that football was supposed to be enjoyable.
That image has become almost mythical.
(It’s also wrong - or least it was for the 1990s onwards. Great players, not great teams; and even the finalists of 1998 or the winners in 2002 were pretty dour - coasting on the dreams of previous generations)
Still, today’s Brazil side, say critics, feels different. 
It’s more organised, more professional, more athletic - in other words less Brazilian. 
No one is reaching for the samba music, for the fireworks or the cold beers.
The online argument is that evangelical Christianity, which has grown dramatically over the past forty years across South America, encourages discipline, restraint and moral seriousness in ways that sit uneasily with the carnival spirit once associated with Brazilian football.
Whether or not that diagnosis is fair is another matter. It is also worth remembering that every generation complains that the next one has lost something precious.
But what fascinates me is that so many of these discussions eventually trace their origins back five hundred years - to an Augustinian monk in Wittenberg.
Martin Luther was a man not short of belief in his own brilliance (as Afua Hirsch and I discuss in two episodes of Legacy a few months ago).
Here’s a link if you fancy listening [ https://substack.com/redirect/70d76641-8d6c-4e99-9b85-057a42b51e05?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] (and do please sign up to Legacy +! [ https://substack.com/redirect/6581711c-a328-4a24-ad82-3a9a7801db0d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ])
So if you’d told him his name would one day be invoked to explain a Brazilian World Cup exit 500 years later, I suspect he would not have been surprised.
Luther was the primary catalyst of what became the Protestant Reformation, challenging papal authority, attacking indulgences and insisting that salvation came through faith alone. His ideas transformed Europe, fractured Christendom and helped trigger more than a century of religious conflict.
He was a giant of history.
He was also astonishingly abrasive, rude and pleased with himself (I strongly recommend the magnificent Martin Luther: Renegade and Prophet by my colleague and friend Lyndal Roper)
Luther had lots of legacies; but one was that he has long attracted acolytes whose caused imploded. Prussia embraced Luther as a foundational national hero in the nineteenth century when he became a symbol of German unity, discipline and national destiny. 
Statues multiplied; anniversaries became patriotic festivals; schoolchildren learned to see him not merely as a religious reformer but as the father of the German nation. That didn’t end too well.
Luther was a big hit too with the Nazis who celebrated Luther as a great German figure for the same reason - and also because his virulently anti-Jewish writings chimed with their own views. 
That did not mean Luther was responsible for the rise of Hitler, although it is striking that in the 1930s, the Nazi Party consistently won far stronger electoral support in parts of Germany with Protestant majorities than Catholic ones, while research by historians like Karina Urbach has shown that Germany's Protestant aristocracy played a significant role in smoothing Hitler's path to power.
None of this, of course, proves anything about Brazil’s football team.
For one thing, millions of evangelical Christians in Brazil would reject the caricature that faith somehow extinguishes joy, music or creativity. 
Some of Brazil’s greatest contemporary footballers are openly evangelical while remaining wonderfully expressive players. Correlation is not causation. 
And of course, footballing success depends on coaching, talent development, demographics, economics and luck far more than theology.
Nor should we romanticise the past. 
Pelé’s Brazil side - not least in 1970 - was not all about parties, sex, booze and dancing; they trained ferociously. The idea that previous generations simply turned up, had fun and brushed other teams to one side is a comforting myth.
And of course the strong Lutheran spine of the Norwegian team does not seem to have caused too many problems this time round - not to mention the Germans, who have won the Cup three more times than (broadly Protestant) England.
And yet myths matter.
For one thing, they tell us how societies understand themselves. So my main takeaway is not whether this debate has any substance to it, but that it has ignited discussion in Brazil about identity, national character and history. 
Rather than arguing over expected goals or possession statistics, training methods or tactical decisions, they are debating the Reformation, religious culture and the shadows of history.
We historians spend our lives (well, part of them) watching people use the past to make sense of the present. Sometimes they are right. 
Sometimes they are wildly wrong. More often, they are doing something much more interesting - namely searching for stories that explain what is happening in the world.
Am I convinced that Brazil were knocked out because of Martin Luther or because of evangelical protestantism?
No.
But I have been tickled by the fact that so many people seem to think the Reformation deserves at least part of the blame. 
Sometimes the most revealing thing about a football tournament is not what happens on the pitch, but the histories people tell themselves afterwards.
Thanks for subscribing; if you do enjoy these posts, do please share them - they are fun to do but take a bit of time; so knowing people want to read them makes all the difference.
More soon.
Global Threads with Peter Frankopan is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

ddd9ce11bebf52d1.subject:
Pourquoi est-il si difficile de savoir si un logement est une bouilloire
 thermique?
ddd9ce11bebf52d1.body:
View this post on the web at https://alexispoulin.substack.com/p/pourquoi-est-il-si-difficile-de-savoir

Pourquoi est-il si difficile de savoir si un logement est une bouilloire thermique?
Il n'existe pas aujourd'hui d'indicateur reconnu qui permet de dire si un logement est ou non une bouilloire thermique, malgré les demandes des associations. C’est d’ailleurs à une association, la Fondation pour le Logement, qu’on doit ce terme de bouilloire thermique, formulé pour la première fois en 2023. 
L’objectif de l’association est de créer une notion miroir des passoires thermiques, ces habitations mal isolées et très difficiles à chauffer en hiver. Il existe bien quelques indicateurs concernant l’habitabilité des logements en cas de canicule, mais ils ne permettent pas de définir précisément si une maison ou un appartement devient invivable par fortes chaleurs. 
Premier de ces baromètres : l’indicateur de confort d’été passif (hors climatisation), matérialisé par trois pictogrammes vert, orange et rouge pour un confort bon, moyen ou insuffisant. Ce baromètre prend en compte la présence de volets aux fenêtres et de brasseurs d’airs l’inertie du logement, c’est-à-dire le fait que les murs conservent ou non la fraîcheur, mais aussi l’inertie du logement, c’est-à-dire le fait que les murs conservent ou non la fraîcheur ou encore l’isolation du toit.
À l’aide de cet indicateur, une étude de l’IGNES (Industriels du génie numérique, énergétique et sécuritaire) conclut que près d’un logement sur deux en France présente un mauvais confort d’été et donc, est susceptible d’être une bouilloire thermique, notamment parce qu’il ne comporte pas de volets.
Mais l’indicateur de confort d’été passif a ses limites, notamment parce qu’il n’évalue pas l’environnement dans lequel se trouve le logement. Par ailleurs, ce baromètre ne distingue pas des logements inconfortables d’habitations carrément dangereuses. Enfin, ce baromètre est seulement indicatif et n’est pas contraignant pour les propriétaires.
Il existe aussi un indicateur contraignant à l’intérieur de la réglementation environnementale que doivent obligatoirement respecter les entreprises du bâtiment : le degré-heure. Cet indicateur établit une température maximale au-delà de laquelle un logement devient inconfortable : 26 degrés la nuit et 28 degrés la journée. Le baromètre compte chaque degré de dépassement de ces températures pour chaque heure du jour et de la nuit.
En dessous de 350 degrés par heure sur une année, le logement est confortable en cas de canicule. Le seuil maximum varie selon la taille du logement, la possibilité d’ouvrir ou non les fenêtres et la zone géographique du logement. 
Problème de cet indicateur : il ne concerne que les logements en construction, du parc neuf et non du parc existant. Les dépassements de température sont évalués à partir de simulations sur le futur logement. 
Les associations comme la Fondation pour le logement réclament donc un indicateur adapté pour définir précisément les bouilloires thermiques et contraindre les propriétaires à y faire des travaux. La Fondation pour le logement a d’ailleurs formulé une proposition de loi, Zéro logement bouilloire, déposé en 2025 par 150 députés de huit groupes politiques. Aucune mesure de ce texte n’a été reprise dans le Projet de loi visant la relance et la décentralisation du logement du gouvernement, débattu en ce moment au Sénat.
Notre publication est financée uniquement par ses lecteurs. Pour recevoir les prochains contenus du Monde Moderne, devenez abonné, gratuit ou payant.
Le projet de loi sur l’usage des armes à feu par les forces de l’ordre suscite un tollé
“Un permis de tuer XXL” pour La France insoumise, “une loi liberticide” pour le Parti socialiste. La proposition de loi visant à créer une présomption d’usage légitime des armes pour les policiers et gendarmes suscite un tollé politique à gauche, et pas seulement. Dans un hémicycle surchauffé, le texte a été approuvé à 313 voix contre 199, avec le soutien du gouvernement, d’une très large majorité des députés de son camp (Renaissance-MoDem-Horizons-LR), et de l’alliance Rassemblement national (RN)-Union des droites pour la République (UDR).
Lors d’une séance particulièrement tendue, les députés de gauche ont tenté d’empêcher le vote, à coups de centaines d’amendements déposés sur le fil. Pour contourner cette tentative d’obstruction, le gouvernement a déclenché l’article 44.3 de la Constitution, plus connu sous le nom de “vote bloqué”, qui permet de voter seulement les amendements “proposés ou acceptés par le gouvernement”. Le tout sous les huées de la gauche et les applaudissements de députés de la majorité et du RN.
Porté par le député LR des Alpes-Maritimes Eric Pauget, le texte est inspiré d’une proposition de campagne de Jean-Marie Le Pen dans son programme présidentiel de 2007. Elle a ensuite été reprise par Nicolas Sarkozy, Marine Le Pen puis Eric Zemmour. 
L'objectif est d'en finir avec “la présomption de culpabilité qui pèse sur les forces de l'ordre lorsqu'elles font feu sur un individu”, avance le député, qui souhaite “inverser la charge de la preuve”. 
De nombreux acteurs institutionnels s’opposent à cette proposition de loi. Le point de départ juridique de cette contestation étant que la Cour européenne des droits de l’homme impose à l’Etat, et non à la victime, de démontrer “le caractère indispensable de son recours à la force létale”.
Ce principe serait donc totalement inversé par le texte soutenu par le gouvernement, alors que la France a déjà été condamnée à plusieurs reprises par la CEDH pour “violation du droit à la vie”, qui figure dans l’article 2 de la Convention européenne des droits de l’homme.
Dans une prise de position publiée le 30 juin, Amnesty International dénonce “une bascule historique”, estimant que “cette loi porte atteinte à l’Etat de droit”. Dans son avis du 26 juin, la Défenseure des droits, Claire Hédon, s’alarme d’un “signal dangereux pouvant conduire à la banalisation de l’usage de la force létale et à une érosion de la confiance entre police et population”.
L'inquiétude des détracteurs du texte se concentre notamment sur les enquêtes, diligentées en cas de suspicions de violences policières, qui n'aboutissent déjà pas toujours. C'est l'avis de la députée communiste Elsa Faucillon, qui a regretté à la tribune de l'Assemblée que “trop d'affaires ont installé l'idée que lorsque la force publique tue, l'accès à la justice est plus difficile, les enquêtes plus longues, les responsabilités plus compliquées à établir”.
Avec cette loi, “le fonctionnaire ayant fait usage de son arme à feu ne pourrait plus être placé en garde à vue, ce qui fera obstacle au recueil de sa version, à la réunion des premiers indices et, in fine, à la manifestation de la vérité”, s’alarme par ailleurs un collectif regroupant notamment le Syndicat des avocats de France, celui de la magistrature ou encore la Ligue des droits de l’homme, dans un communiqué commun.
La proposition de loi doit encore passer au Sénat, dominé par la droite. L’auteur du texte espère obtenir un vote “conforme” des sénateurs “pour éviter la navette parlementaire” et que la loi soit adoptée dans la foulée. Si c’est le cas, seul un recours au Conseil constitutionnel pourrait permettre d’annuler ou remanier le texte voté par les deux chambres du Parlement.

Le président turc Recep Tayyip Erdogan a offert en cadeau aux autres dirigeants présents au sommet de l’Otan un pistolet et des munitions, a affirmé le Premier ministre britannique Keir Starmer.
Le dirigeant, qui s’exprimait auprès des journalistes britanniques présents dans son avion de retour d’Ankara, a expliqué que le président turc avait offert à chaque dirigeant un pistolet gravé à son nom. Le tout accompagné d’une boîte de munitions. Le chef d’Etat turc a également joint à son présent une note dispensant les armes des contrôles à l’expor

6d250e8436c76bad.subject: Grok x Cursor
6d250e8436c76bad.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.bensbites.com/p/grok-x-cursor

Hey folks,
Anthropic extended access to Fable 5 via Claude subscriptions until July 12. Same deal as before: up to 50% of your weekly limits, and after July 12, use prepaid usage credits. But this dangling-a-carrot move doesn’t make me trust their deadlines anymore.
Otoh, OpenAI announced that GPT-5.6 models (Sol, Terra and Luna) will be live for all users later today. General feedback on X (from early testers) is that Sol is not smarter than Fable but is so much more reliable that you would not want to use anything else.
Well, let’s see.
Ben’s Bites is brought to you by Flodesk [ https://substack.com/redirect/4fc17669-f990-438c-8a06-ec510ee445fc?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
Most AI tools generate emails for you. Flodesk Studio designs with you — describe what you want, get a beautiful on-brand email in seconds, then refine in chat or perfect by hand. Real designer foundations, AI-accelerated, finished by you. Try it. [ https://substack.com/redirect/4fc17669-f990-438c-8a06-ec510ee445fc?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
Headlines
We have a good Grok model - Grok 4.5 [ https://substack.com/redirect/6d53d542-7c32-4a78-a152-5b8b2703523a?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]. SpaceXAI and Cursor trained it together, and it’s an Opus-class model. It should perform roughly in between Opus 4.7 and 4.8. But it’s much cheaper on a per-token basis and token-efficient—making it 6x cheaper than Opus models and 3x cheaper than GPT-5.5. It’s available in Cursor with increased limits and via API too [ https://substack.com/redirect/65b60156-76ed-4b07-be4d-c0ea397d1bce?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]. 
ChatGPT Voice is finally getting some new models - GPT-Live-1 and Live-1-mini [ https://substack.com/redirect/9bac03d4-9669-4c14-abf9-e1f094f8fd5e?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]. A new architecture lets these models a) talk while you’re still speaking without cutting your message off and b) use GPT-5.5 in the background. Results in much smoother conversations and a big jump in intelligence from the old advanced voice mode.
A lighter version of Claude Cowork will work on web and mobile [ https://substack.com/redirect/8f5cb0d8-aa3d-4921-8e0c-89469d75f0d1?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - sessions will run remotely and save to your account, so you can kick off a task on the laptop, close it, and check in from your phone. Cowork on the desktop app remains the full deal. Beta rolls out over the coming weeks.
Muse Image and Video [ https://substack.com/redirect/b1d277ce-2b47-4da9-b0c4-7e856d441014?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - New multimedia generation models from Meta. The images have more of an AI look, but the model seems good at image editing and text rendering, and can search the web to get facts right, etc. They also have a video model in preview that will be released soon.

01822f89794b82b7.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.lennysnewsletter.com/p/adam-mosseri-ai-is-a-tailwind-for

Adam Mosseri is the Head of Instagram, where he oversees an app used by over 3 billion people. He also leads the team building Threads. Adam has run Instagram for longer than its founders did, after taking over from Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger in 2018. A designer by training, he spent over 15 years at Meta, starting as a designer on Facebook’s mobile app, rising to lead Facebook’s News Feed, and eventually chosen to lead Instagram. During his tenure, Instagram’s user base has more than tripled.
Listen on YouTube [ https://substack.com/redirect/51d6d84c-1095-425c-b40a-7e70dd77322a?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], Spotify [ https://substack.com/redirect/4b131ae2-ef19-4937-a7cc-c30d896de2a6?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], and Apple Podcasts [ https://substack.com/redirect/f7ec3f6a-6601-41d5-9407-eef1cb03f326?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
In our in-depth conversation, we discuss:
How the canonical product team structure is changing in 2026, from baker’s-dozen specialist teams to lean pods of four to six generalists
The rise of the “product staff” role—a blending of PM, design, data science, and research into one generalist operator
Why Adam is bullish on designers even as functional boundaries dissolve, and which roles are most at risk
What the Instagram algorithm knows about you, and why it’s only now catching up to what people assumed it knew years ago
Why the rise of AI-generated content is a tailwind for Instagram, and how the company is thinking about creator identity in a synthetic-content world
The two biggest product failures of Adam’s career—Facebook Home and the first version of Reels
Brought to you by:
WorkOS [ https://substack.com/redirect/5bdfa078-4a5a-47bc-a533-3ad3d502b800?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]—Make your app enterprise-ready, with SSO, SCIM, RBAC, and more

77c70b1f786cf3f3.subject: Platner’s Defiant Departure. A Way Out of the Iran Mess. Plus. . .
77c70b1f786cf3f3.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.thefp.com/p/platners-defiant-departure-a-way

It’s Thursday, July 9. This is The Front Page, your daily window into the world of The Free Press—and our take on the world at large. Today: Eli Lake on Graham Platner’s attempt to make himself a martyr. Inside the 2028 Republican shadow primary. The immigration hard-liners suing the Trump administration over deportations. And much more.
But first: The war with Iran is officially back on.
At the NATO summit in Turkey yesterday, President Donald Trump declared the memorandum of understanding (MOU) with Iran dead. Iran attacked three commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, triggering a wave of U.S. strikes that continued into Wednesday. Trump reimposed the naval blockade and said he wasn’t sure he wanted a deal at all. “We can play games,” he told reporters [ https://substack.com/redirect/13b528fe-05b8-4023-9f3f-69c8cef855ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], “but I’m not sure I want to make a deal. Let’s just finish the job.”
So where do we go from here? Aaron MacLean argues that the administration’s original sin was failing to anticipate Iran’s most obvious countermove: closing the Strait of Hormuz. The ceasefire agreed upon three weeks ago was built on self-deception, with the United States claiming that the MOU required Iran to open the strait when its text said nothing of the sort. Now, with the agreement dead and strikes resuming, Trump faces four choices. Read Aaron on what those options are, and why only one of them offers a real way out.
Meanwhile, as millions gather in Iranian cities to mourn the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iranian dissident Masih Alinejad has been hearing a very different story from people inside the country. What the Islamic Republic is staging as a display of strength, she argues, is instead a performance. Read Masih on what’s really happening on the ground.
—The Editors
MORE FROM THE FREE PRESS
THE NEWS
The Trump administration cleared OpenAI [ https://substack.com/redirect/4aaa016a-1da3-4052-b2ca-b432dbb1acf5?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] to release its most powerful AI model, GPT-5.6, with a public launch set for today. The move follows a government-mandated staggered rollout last month of the same model, which restricted access to approved entities while officials conducted additional testing.
A federal judge ordered yesterday that Trump must hand over [ https://substack.com/redirect/23a530ee-e0af-4adb-8558-cd927f657bee?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] a $5 million judgment to writer E. Jean Carroll, after the Supreme Court declined to hear his appeal last week. A Manhattan jury awarded Carroll the sum in 2023 after finding Trump liable for sexually assaulting and defaming her. Trump’s lawyers have filed a rehearing request and said they would appeal the order.
Kentucky governor Andy Beshear sent a letter [ https://substack.com/redirect/e72ceb66-5028-4eea-9bc2-dafc7ed07a29?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] to Senator Mitch McConnell’s office on Wednesday, urging him to be transparent about his health after 24 days of silence from the 84-year-old. With rampant speculation circulating about his condition, Beshear said his constituents deserve answers about whether McConnell can continue to serve.
At the NATO summit in Turkey, Trump said the U.S. will allow Ukraine to manufacture [ https://substack.com/redirect/de8b580e-d392-454b-8235-c84d708a4a87?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] Patriot air defense systems by granting it a production license—a significant win for Kyiv, which has been pressing for the capability for years. Trump met with President Volodymyr Zelensky on the sidelines of the summit, praising him as having “done an amazing job” in the war.
The family of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Mexican national shot and killed by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer during a Houston immigration raid Tuesday, is calling for an independent investigation [ https://substack.com/redirect/71deea20-bbb8-4aa3-b36b-60ed6f21087a?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] into his death. The Department of Homeland Security said the agent fired in self-defense after Salgado Araujo attempted to ram an officer with a vehicle.
Former Milwaukee County judge Hannah Dugan was fined $5,000 [ https://substack.com/redirect/e7846cd1-1e4b-4247-873c-f1d13c76523f?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] but spared prison after being convicted of obstructing the arrest of an undocumented immigrant. The penalty falls well below the 15- to 21-month sentence in the federal guidelines.
Egypt filed an official complaint [ https://substack.com/redirect/2409964a-07ab-4081-aa37-927878f3e300?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] with FIFA after blowing a 2–0 lead in the final minutes of its World Cup game against Argentina, alleging that inconsistent Video Assistant Referee (VAR) decisions directly influenced the outcome.
Emmy nominations are out [ https://substack.com/redirect/83b20774-1c22-4e4e-b262-218478cd49ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], and HBO Max’s medical drama The Pitt led all shows with 25 nods. The final season of Hacks set a comedy record with 24 nominations. A win for Jean Smart in September would tie the all-time record for acting Emmys by a single performer.

**The Rundown: **SpaceXAI just [launched](https://x.ai/news/grok-4-5) Grok 4.5, the first model it built jointly with Cursor after its $60B acquisition, featuring strong benchmarks, speed, and efficiency gains that undercut frontier pricing and vault Grok back into the AI conversation. 

**The details:**

* 4.5 features strong performance on coding, agentic, and knowledge work tasks, with benchmarks in similar ranges to Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5. 

* The model runs at 80 tokens per second, a speed that rivals flash models, while claiming a 4x efficiency gain over models like Opus 4.8. 

* Pricing lands at $2/$6 per million input/output tokens, versus $5/$25 for Opus 4.8, with usage temporarily free inside Cursor and Grok Build.

* Elon Musk [pitched](https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2074740539874775163?s=20) the release on X as "an Opus-class model, but faster, more token-efficient and lower cost”, with an even bigger model [coming](https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2074893922812252354?s=20) next month.

**Why it matters: **Grok has historically been somewhat of a punchline compared to the frontier, but 4.5 came to play — with very impressive performance at this speed, price, and efficiency. With Cursor’s Composer models also continuing to move up the coding ladder, the gains from the $60B acquisition look to be flowing in both directions.

**The Rundown: **OpenAI just [rolled out](https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-live/) GPT-Live, its next-gen voice model that makes talking to AI "feel much more like having a real conversation", featuring an architecture that removes the clunky nature of previous experiences. 

**The details: **

* Live is a "full-duplex" architecture, processing inputs continuously instead of old systems that strung together models and used turn-based conversation.

* When a question needs more reasoning, Live can delegate to a stronger model for the background work, and also surface visual cards alongside answers.

* The ability to listen, process, and output simultaneously leads to less clunky, awkward pauses, while enabling [abilities](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K-fYBO8t3-A) like real-time translation.

* In OAI's tests, users picked GPT-Live over the old Advanced Voice Mode in around 3 of 4 head-to-head chats, with expert science scores nearly doubling. 

**Why it matters: **Sam Altman has often talked about his voice vision from the movie ‘Her’, but previous models hit snags that removed the natural feel. Live’s ability to talk, reason, and listen at the same time gives Voice the futuristic feel it deserves, especially at a time when more users than ever are leaning towards speaking over typing.

98f77db7359a2a83.subject: The Risks and Rewards of Chinese AI
98f77db7359a2a83.body:
View this post on the web at https://linguasinica.substack.com/p/the-risks-and-rewards-of-chinese

Late last month, an op-ed published in the German magazine  [ https://substack.com/redirect/4e7f8086-bd3e-4ef6-ad00-de23f41e3885?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]Focus+ [ https://substack.com/redirect/4e7f8086-bd3e-4ef6-ad00-de23f41e3885?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] argued that Europe should embrace open-source models from China — those offered free of charge for anyone to download, modify, and run — as a transitional plan to develop its own AI models. Written by AI expert Jürgen Seitz, the opinion piece came shortly after the US government briefly suspended access by non-US users to Anthropic’s Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, raising fears in Europe [ https://substack.com/redirect/88a668b9-28e1-483d-8002-4d1ad90624be?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] and the UK [ https://substack.com/redirect/880b0eb5-c685-4688-8910-3321277c565a?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] about vulnerability stemming from over-reliance on US tech.
In the commentary, which was the focus of a German-press roundup published by the Chinese-language service of Germany’s Deutsche Welle [ https://substack.com/redirect/2ce3cb26-b845-4fc3-9eec-3d6f0883accd?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], Seitz argued that the US ban serves as a warning sign for Europe that embracing US-made AI models is not a one-off solution if it wants to develop its own AI models. Meanwhile, he said, the performance of GLM-5.2 [ https://substack.com/redirect/e4987b25-c6e8-44ee-89b0-0b5eb7ce11a0?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], the model launched by the Chinese-backed Zhipu AI, is approaching that of US-made AI models. 
Seitz holds  [ https://substack.com/redirect/b2c80f72-47ed-4f52-b0ad-77445c2c50fb?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]the Hubert Burda Media-endowed professorship in AI for digital media and business models at Germany’s Offenburg University.
The Focus+ commentary was picked up over the past week by Chinese state media to promote the benefits of China’s AI models. A piece by the official China News Service [ https://substack.com/redirect/c63abddf-f0f5-4de8-8455-0c14e15a9dc2?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] on July 10, re-posted by news portals such as Sina Finance [ https://substack.com/redirect/30ad56de-c6b8-474d-a3d1-3b8d62ada1af?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] and Henan’s Dahe Online [ https://substack.com/redirect/5d63e717-8987-49d5-ba9f-49abf4f67db4?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], said Europe now has “another development path” (另一条发展路径) between depending on American AI and waiting for homegrown models to mature. The piece also cited a European Think-tank Network on China report [ https://substack.com/redirect/d0490faf-9c9f-4e52-8533-21af0a813ca4?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] from last month, characterizing it as urging Brussels to balance stronger support for its own tech sector with pragmatic cooperation with China. That report, however, is more cautious than state media coverage suggests — noting the shift in the EU toward greater “de-risking” and concluding that “[distrust] in Chinese technology will stay.”
Risk and Reward
The GLM-5.2 model is now ranked fourth according to the latest LLM Leaderboard provided by OpenRouter [ https://substack.com/redirect/1aa55355-362b-4917-92dc-c3baa9d24e8c?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], where eight Chinese AI models appear on the list. The model is also attractive for its low cost.
Much media coverage in recent weeks has reported on GLM-5.2 as closing the gap with more expensive US AI offerings. Reuters reported [ https://substack.com/redirect/4a6fd787-afe2-4de2-b475-861472a070b3?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] on July 2 that the model now sits in fifth place on Artificial Analysis’ intelligence leaderboard while running at roughly a sixth of the cost of comparable US models.
But a turn to Chinese AI alternatives would entail other risks. 
A previous report by CMP, “DeepSeeking Truth [ https://substack.com/redirect/aea1ad31-4fdb-4532-bfe1-7c3fca696b2e?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ],” found that, rather than simply removing information deemed unfavorable, China has adapted its longstanding methods of “public opinion guidance [ https://substack.com/redirect/69ab9b26-c280-4191-81a5-ed83e49a5f09?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]” (舆论导向), a technique for shaping public narratives that goes beyond simple censorship and dates back to the 1989 Tiananmen Massacre [ https://substack.com/redirect/49608acf-6c3d-43bd-8cb5-7c8c29b56198?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], to the regulation and creation of AI.
A more recent CMP report, “Guided Intelligence [ https://substack.com/redirect/b3453f93-aea8-4b09-8737-424a21e231ac?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ],” found developers building on Chinese foundation models struggled to remove embedded information guidance, and that several models were also vulnerable to jailbreaking, yielding information on explosives and poisons.
Unlike Mythos, which Anthropic has kept restricted to vetted organizations under Project Glasswing [ https://substack.com/redirect/f4ff4722-ffe3-496c-8126-bc7dd721efb9?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], GLM-5.2 can be downloaded and run locally without built-in content moderation — a gap that researchers cited by Forbes [ https://substack.com/redirect/38db54de-fa8b-4603-bb85-e2490d1043f6?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] say makes it “good enough” for offensive cyber use at a fraction of the cost, even without matching Mythos’s full capability.
Seitz acknowledges in his Focus+ commentary that Chinese AI models could pose risks of their own. He says European countries should deploy Chinese AI models on European servers, rather than “uncontrollable” Chinese servers.
Zhipu AI was added to the trade restrictions blacklist [ https://substack.com/redirect/cb0f707b-b139-4831-a83e-51fce08ade6d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] maintained by the US Department of Commerce last year. This week, Reuters reported [ https://substack.com/redirect/246bca57-6f71-4fdc-9b43-87fc39922b88?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] that the Chinese government invited domestic AI companies, including Zhipu, to meetings discussing potential restrictions on overseas access, a sign that Beijing, too, treats AI as a “national asset.”
Putting Z.Ai to the (Basic) Test
To gauge how Zhipu AI responds to sensitive material, Lingua Sinica put several questions to the earlier GLM-4.7 model. It was consistently unable to answer questions touching on topics sensitive to the Chinese government — the opposite of its detailed responses to general queries, such as “What preparations should I make before a typhoon?” or “Recommend tourist attractions in Shanghai.” Like DeepSeek, GLM-4.7 shows its “thought process,” typically six or seven steps, before generating an answer; on sensitive questions, it tends to stop both thinking and generating the moment it identifies a sensitive element, such as a mention of activists.
Seitz acknowledges that Chinese AI models could pose risks of their own.
On Taiwan, Z.ai [ https://substack.com/redirect/610fa78c-31b6-4c44-ba91-8a512eeb48b4?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] declined to generate a reply at all, instead reciting the official line that the island is “an insepara

fc155fea3aff6537.subject: Revirement inattendu dans la stratégie IA de la Chine
fc155fea3aff6537.body:
View this post on the web at https://iaethiqueinsider.substack.com/p/revirement-inattendu-dans-la-strategie

Chers Insiders,
Selon Reuters, repris début juillet par Fortune [ https://substack.com/redirect/a4f1c8c8-e6b9-4ce9-8db1-6214989063ef?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], le ministère du Commerce chinois a réuni Alibaba, ByteDance et la start-up Z.ai (qui édite le modèle GLM) pour discuter de verrouiller l’accès étranger à leurs meilleurs modèles : Qwen, Doubao et GLM-5.2, y compris des versions à poids ouverts, qu’on télécharge et inspecte librement une fois publiées. Les pistes : interdire la publication de certains modèles hors de Chine, qualifier la fuite d’un modèle propriétaire d’atteinte à la sécurité nationale, limiter les investisseurs autorisés à les financer. Rien n’est acté, aucun ministère n’a commenté. Mais l’intention suffit à m’alerter.
Ce que je n’avais pas anticipé, c’est que Pékin envisage cette option. Jusqu’ici, la stratégie chinoise misait sur l’inverse : inonder le monde de modèles à poids ouverts et gratuits pour gagner en influence là où les modèles américains restent fermés et facturés à l’usage. Qwen, Doubao et GLM ont ainsi conquis des millions d’utilisateurs occidentaux. Y renoncer reviendrait, pour la Chine, à adopter le même réflexe de contrôle que Washington a activé en juin en coupant l’accès mondial à Fable 5 et Mythos. Concrètement, il existe désormais deux catégories de modèles : ceux qu’un État peut fermer du jour au lendemain, et ceux qu’aucun gouvernement ne peut couper à lui seul.
Cette deuxième catégorie n’a rien de théorique. Hugging Face (la plateforme communautaire où se partagent la plupart des modèles ouverts, chinois comme américains) héberge des poids sous licences qui ne dépendent d’aucun gouvernement. C’est ce que j’utilise dans mon propre travail : une gouvernance distribuée entre des milliers de contributeurs, d’entreprises et de chercheurs, qu’aucun ministère ne peut convoquer un vendredi soir pour couper l’accès.
Encore faut-il financer cette couche. Preuve cette semaine : la start-up parisienne ZML, fondée par Steeve Morin (cofondateur de Zenly), a mis en ligne LLMD [ https://substack.com/redirect/dec6bbed-1d86-4761-91dd-489b6a8bf049?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], un serveur gratuit qui fait tourner Qwen, Gemma, Mistral ou LLaMA indifféremment sur des puces Nvidia, AMD, Intel ou Google. Sur sa table de capital figurent Clément Delangue et Julien Chaumond, les fondateurs de Hugging Face. Voilà le type de brique qu’il faut soutenir : elle rend un modèle ouvert utilisable sur n’importe quel matériel, sans dépendre d’un seul fournisseur de puces ni d’un seul État.
Jacob Helberg, sous-secrétaire d’État américain aux Affaires économiques, défend cette idée dans une tribune récente [ https://substack.com/redirect/c0221cfe-c14e-4ee4-b194-15556a063b7d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] : vouloir reconstruire chez soi une pile technologique qui existe déjà ailleurs relève de la stagnation synchronisée, pas de la souveraineté. Sur ce point précis, je suis d’accord avec lui : je ne crois pas non plus que l’Europe doive tout reconstruire elle-même. Mieux vaut miser sur des niches où on peut vraiment percer, comme la robotique avec Robostral Navigate de Mistral, et les coupler à des briques ouvertes et agnostiques comme celle de ZML. Un poids ouvert téléchargé aujourd’hui reste utilisable indéfiniment, mais rien ne garantit que le suivant le sera : si Pékin referme son robinet, seul notre stock continue de tourner. Raison de plus pour ne pas faire reposer notre accès à l’intelligence de pointe sur la constance d’un seul camp, ouvert ou fermé, ni sur la générosité d’une coalition écrite à Washington.
Cette semaine, je me demande surtout ce qu’on construit, nous, pour que l’accès aux meilleurs modèles cesse de dépendre du bon vouloir d’un seul pays. Chaque euro mis dans une infrastructure ouverte et interopérable comme celle de ZML vaut mieux qu’un pari sur la bienveillance de Washington ou de Pékin.
Ce jeu de contrôle ne se limite pas aux modèles, il touche aussi vos données. Dans le nouvel épisode d’IA Éthique Insider, je reçois de nouveau Sidy Juste, avocat aux barreaux de Paris et de Montréal, pour décrypter l’arrêt Trump v. Slaughter du 29 juin : la Cour suprême américaine y a fait tomber l’indépendance de la FTC, l’autorité censée garantir la confidentialité de vos données une fois transférées aux États-Unis. Sidy Juste explique pourquoi il ne voit « pas comment [l’accord qui encadre ces transferts] pourrait survivre à l’année 2026 », et ce que l’Europe prépare de son côté avec son nouveau Tech Sovereignty Package. À regarder juste après votre lecture.
Enfin, grâce à Fable j’ai mis-à-jour mon site web si jamais cela réveille votre curiosité je suis preneuse de vos retours : www.daphneelucenet.com .
Bonne lecture et à la semaine prochaine !
🪢 Daphnée d’IA Éthique Insider
Suivez-moi sur LinkedIn [ https://substack.com/redirect/002dfa90-91fc-4252-959a-fedcce3ccb6c?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], Instagram [ https://substack.com/redirect/51ad7502-e60a-415f-9c42-955b1b0a19e9?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], Youtube [ https://substack.com/redirect/49989450-8d10-4141-9834-64c4611a976f?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
P.S. Les résumés sont générés par IA et corrigés par mes soins.
News de la semaine en bref
🤓 Général
OpenAI ouvre GPT-5.6 après douze jours sous contrôle du gouvernement américain (lecture de 5 minutes) [ https://substack.com/redirect/90513f18-fb4c-4dac-b557-2b28a35c5a3d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
OpenAI a mis en accès public le 9 juillet sa nouvelle famille de modèles, GPT-5.6, en trois versions : Sol (le plus puissant, pour le code et la recherche complexe), Terra (usage courant) et Luna (rapide et économique). Avant cette ouverture, Sol a été réservé douze jours à une vingtaine d’organisations validées une par une par le département du Commerce américain, le temps que son agence de sécurité IA les examine. Sol facture 5 dollars le million de jetons (l’unité de facturation de l’IA) en entrée. Même un modèle grand public passe désormais par une liste d’autorisations gouvernementale avant sa mise en ligne.
La numéro deux d’OpenAI quitte son poste pour raison de santé (lecture de 4 minutes) [ https://substack.com/redirect/811f4c77-1ed4-4200-bf20-da57936c0597?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
Fidji Simo, directrice des applications d’OpenAI (le poste créé pour elle en 2025, qui régit ChatGPT et les produits grand public), quitte son rôle à temps plein pour devenir conseillère à temps partiel. En congé médical depuis avril pour une rechute d’un syndrome neuro-immunitaire diagnostiqué en 2019 (le POTS, qui provoque vertiges et fatigue intense en position debout), elle explique que son rétablissement s’annonce plus long que prévu. Ancienne dirigeante d’Instacart, elle avait pris ce poste stratégique en mai 2025. Le départ d’une dirigeante de ce rang rappelle que la gouvernance des laboratoires d’IA les plus puissants tient parfois à très peu de personnes.
Tencent rachète Manus à Meta, sous pression de Pékin (lecture de 4 minutes) [ https://substack.com/redirect/c2a8ac14-2561-4218-a1ac-417afdb65086?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
Tencent (le géant chinois du jeu vidéo et des réseaux sociaux, éditeur de l’application WeChat) négocie pour devenir le premier actionnaire de Manus, une start-up d’agents IA (des IA capables d’exécuter des tâches de façon autonome). L’opération intervient après que Pékin a ordonné à Meta de défaire son rachat de Manus à 2 milliards de dollars, conclu quelques mois plus tôt. a7a5f8c1d9e93a62.subject: Why the Left Hates the Medicine That Made Me a Mom
a7a5f8c1d9e93a62.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.thefp.com/cp/207069898

Just over a year ago, I stayed up until 2 a.m. editing a story, because the deadline was harder than most: The writer, Madeleine Kearns, was on the brink of giving birth. And the story in question was about what she had gone through to bring her baby into the world. Many of you read—and loved!—that story, but for those who didn’t: After struggling with pain and infertility, which no mainstream doctor could conclusively explain, Maddy sought out something called “restorative reproductive medicine” in order to conceive her daughter.
It’s a branch of medicine that’s often misrepresented in left-leaning outlets as conservative quackery, because it was pioneered by Catholic surgeons as an alternative to in vitro fertilization (IVF), to which the Church is opposed. Just last week, it was characterized in “The New York Times” as “an approach that many medical experts argue may give false hope to couples who have not been able to have children.” But for Maddy, it felt like her only hope.
And it worked. Her daughter was born exactly a year ago today. (Happy birthday to her!) At the time, Maddy and I spoke a lot about how, even though she sought the treatment because she’s Catholic, it could help so many women who aren’t, for whom IVF isn’t an option. But in her piece today—a response to the “Times” article—Maddy argues that politicizing this treatment could deter people from seeking it. —Freya Sanders
The left-leaning press is wildly suspicious about the medical intervention that allowed me to become a mother.
“A faction of Christian conservatives and MAHA-heads have joined forces to advocate for ‘restorative reproductive medicine,’” warns  [ https://substack.com/redirect/8e59f585-36eb-4ea7-ba82-1b2201c9c2cf?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]The Cut [ https://substack.com/redirect/8e59f585-36eb-4ea7-ba82-1b2201c9c2cf?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], explaining that its proponents operate “on the flawed assumption that infertility can be treated with lifestyle modifications like diet, exercise, and cycle charting.”
Meanwhile the  [ https://substack.com/redirect/1fe88f6a-47a5-43a3-9330-ad74093c59ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]Guardian [ https://substack.com/redirect/1fe88f6a-47a5-43a3-9330-ad74093c59ea?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] wonders if it is “the right’s path to limiting IVF.”
And, just last week, The New York Times described it as [ https://substack.com/redirect/aeb21d7b-ab3b-4bf2-a58e-9903fcb8f848?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] “an approach that many medical experts argue may give false hope to couples who have not been able to have children.”...

Unsubscribe https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly93d3cudGhlZnAuY29tL2FjdGlvbi9kaXNhYmxlX2VtYWlsL2Rpc2FibGU_.FwOfJEKkf6EKxlZzkU4hliOL0K5P4DyC3Xr7mZqfxM0?

671113365380a6d1.subject: Benedict's Newsletter: No. 651
671113365380a6d1.body:
NO. 651   FREE EDITION   SUNDAY 12 JUL 2026
SPONSORED BY MATERIAL
https://ben-evans.us6.list-manage.com/track/click?u=b98e2de85f03865f1d38de74f&id=f0358c1674&e=41a75eb7a5
Email isn't less important. It's just no longer enough to defend on its own. The breach lives in the sessions, the OAuth grants, the password resets and magic links, the files sitting exposed after the click — places inbound filters were never built to watch.

Stop securing email. Start securing the cloud workspace. (https://ben-evans.us6.list-manage.com/track/click?u=b98e2de85f03865f1d38de74f&id=570384e669&e=41a75eb7a5)


** Preview from the Premium edition
------------------------------------------------------------

A decade ago, there was a fashionable idea in Silicon Valley that SaaS meant enterprise software could go direct. Instead of struggling through IT procurement processes and 18-month sales cycles, you could go direct, bottom up, to the grassroots, and your actual users would drive demand. This kind of worked, up to a point, for a handful of companies (Slack, say, or Notion), and ‘shadow IT’ is a real thing. But more generally, this ran into three pretty basic problems, that apply in exactly the same way to generative AI today: most people don’t see the problem to be solved, most people are not tool builders, and most people aren't in a position to build the tool anyway.

First, think for a minute about how often you just didn’t get it the first time you saw something that now you use every day. And you’re probably a product-minded early-adopter - everyone else struggles even more. A large proportion of productivity software solves a problem that the people who are using it didn't realise that they had. This is half of the job of sales.

Second, most people aren't tool builders. There's a difference between being the right customer for a tool and being the right person to build

PREMIUM SUBSCRIBERS GET THE FULL COLUMN EVERY WEEK. TO READ THE REST, UPGRADE HERE. (https://us.list-manage.com/5G1Xg69ujlQ?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)


** My work
------------------------------------------------------------


** Ways to think about token pricing
------------------------------------------------------------

AI is in a supply crunch today, but what happens when we come out of it? How and where will supply, demand, price, capacity and capex get back into equilibrium? Today, model labs can name their price, but why won’t they end up as low-margin commodity infrastructure? LINK (https://us.list-manage.com/Z0ut1UWJB0e?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)


** News
------------------------------------------------------------


** Model week
------------------------------------------------------------

After Anthropic spent a couple of weeks leading the market with Fable, OpenAI caught back up with its new 5.6 models, which are roughly at par. This has been the pattern since the beginning of last year - the top handful of labs jump ahead of each other every month or two, but no-one has a sustainable lead, and there is no clear path to getting one. LINK (https://us.list-manage.com/7y3JEkSvOfK?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)

Proving the point - Anthropic got buzz in tech with CoWork, a software layer building agentic flows with third-party plug-ins, and now OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work, doing much the same thing. The most interesting part is how incredibly chaotic, confused and messy the app experience is - really bad work. Meanwhile, OpenAI’s AI-enabled web browser, Atlas, has been discontinued: we haven’t found the right UX here yet. WORK (https://us.list-manage.com/yorDjgoWYld?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , APP (https://us.list-manage.com/siQBKMmMFb2?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , BROWSER (https://us.list-manage.com/SHw2kkbv4EP?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)

Fidji Simo, OpenAI’s head of product and business (who was a potential replacement for Sam Altman), went on a medical leave of absence earlier this year and has now had to step down entirely. The management structure will change again, again (which might be part of why the new app is such a fiasco). LINK (https://us.list-manage.com/PAZdcfDasbD?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)

Meanwhile, SpaceX/xAI launched a new version of its Grok model that put it back up towards the top of the leader boards, and more importantly at the ‘pareto curve’ - it has a leading price/ performance ratio. And Meta’s new AI lab also launched a new version of its rebuilt model, Muse Spark, which isn’t far behind. Neither of these are quite at the cutting edge for model performance, but they’re competitive or nearly so on price/ performance, which is suddenly much more relevant. Equally, Mark Zuckerberg explicitly claimed the the current models are too expensive - and followed through with his proposal to make his models available in the cloud to developers, competing directly with Gemini, OpenAI and Anthropic for the first time.

To my opening point above - neither of these have yet leapfrogged the leaders, but they’re not that far off, and they’ve managed to get back into the game after failing out last year. It remains entirely unclear what structural barriers to entry there are beyond cash. SPACEX (https://us.list-manage.com/12r7MKqeivd?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , META (https://us.list-manage.com/YlP5flZo3MD?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , META CLOUD (https://us.list-manage.com/OUXPKIrWfoJ?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)


** Apple versus OpenAI
------------------------------------------------------------

In 2024 Jony Ive, Tang Tan (a former senior hardware exec at Apple) and a bunch of other mostly-former Apple people founded Io, a startup planning to build an AI device. In 2025 OpenAI bought Io, for $6.5bn, and made Tang Tan head of hardware. And this week, Apple sued Tang Tan, OpenAI and Chang Liu, another former Apple employee also now at OpenAI.

Apple claims that Tang Tan took Apple proprietary data when he left and told Apple employees with job interviews at OpenAI to take ’actual parts’ from Apple’s office and bring them to OpenAI. It claims that Chang Liu not only took Apple data before leaving, but continued to access Apple systems after he left and took more data (he had access because of a bug), and coached other Apple employees joining OpenAI to take more Apple data before leaving, and coached them on how to avoid Apple security checks.

I’m obviously not a lawyer, and some parts of this seem more blatantly problematic than others (and God knows what will come out in discovery), but some of the externalities are interesting. Apple doesn’t mention Jony Ive at all. It does mention that over 400 former Apple engineers now work at OpenAI. And none of this is good for OpenAI’s plans for a consumer hardware device, assuming that’s still live anyway, nor for an IPO. LINK (https://us.list-manage.com/3bq5Cenp0Vf?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , IO/LOVEFROM (https://us.list-manage.com/j0DKNNfrmwc?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)


** AI export wars
------------------------------------------------------------

Reuters says Beijing is considering export controls on open-source models, which have seen a surge in interest in the US give the pricing crunch (OpenRouter, with a sample so skewed I’m generally reluctant to reference it, says Deepseek and Qwen alone are 25% of its usage). As I wrote in the essay linked above, all of the variables for token pricing are in play right now. LINK (https://us.list-manage.com/11xqnlAtZ_p?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b) , OPENROUTER (https://us.list-manage.com/ywOtMpln4kc?e=41a75eb7a5&c2id=5fbae51e103c78da448dcb114e086e9b)

On the other side of this trade, meanwhile, the FT found Op

32876e959ee408e9.subject: You Can't Opine on AI From the Cheap Seats
32876e959ee408e9.body:
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/421cfc90-9444-46d3-a133-4ed7b4d3245e/Newsletter_Thumbnails__1200_x_630_px_.png?t=1784003534)
Caption: 

———————————————————————————

**Coming Soon: The 1752vc Pitch Deck Analyzer **

Upload your deck, get investor-grade feedback in minutes — trained on 25,000+ real decks. Launching soon on Product Hunt.

👉 Follow to get notified:[ ](https://www.producthunt.com/products/1752vc-pitch-deck-analysis?launch=1752vc-pitch-deck-analyzer)**[Follow on Product Hunt](https://www.producthunt.com/products/1752vc-pitch-deck-analysis?launch=1752vc-pitch-deck-analyzer)**

———————————————————————————

Read every AI research paper. Listen to every podcast. Nod along at every dinner where someone explains why their portfolio company is "AI-native."

You still won't know what is possible versus what is not.

Not until you've tried to make these models do something hard yourself — and watched them fail, fluently, on your own data.

So we did. We built an AI system to score every deck that comes through 1752 — a multimodal pipeline that reads a deck the way an investor does, with a governance layer encoding how investors judge a company, calibrated against real decisions. Not to ship it. To find out what's real and what's BS — because you can't generate alpha from the cheap seats.

A few convictions came out the other side. Not predictions. Scars.

## The Orchestration/Infrastructure Bet Is the Right One

Getting a multimodal model to do what you actually want — with meaningfully fewer hallucinations — is one hell of a lift.

We knew that abstractly. Now we know it personally.

The teams building those rails are solving the genuinely hard problem. Not the demo problem. The make-it-work-ten-thousand-times-in-a-row problem. The one that doesn't trend and doesn't fit in a keynote.

That's exactly why we want to back the rails. When the hype cycle exhales — and it always does — the companies holding up everyone else's product are the ones still standing.

## Your Proprietary Data Is Your Partners' Judgment, Captured

You better have proprietary data to bend these models to your will. Your own, or sourced. 

We used to say that as a thesis. Now I've lived it.

What's our proprietary data? It's how experienced investors actually evaluate a founder — the reasoning behind a yes, a no, a maybe. You can't buy that. It doesn't exist in a public dataset. We built it: we pulled signal from 25,000 real pitch decks and distilled it into a working set of more than three thousand principles the system scores against.

But raw extraction is just noise with ambition. The hard part — the part that actually took the work — was cleaning it. Cutting the duplicates. Killing the contradictions. Throwing out the principles that sounded sharp and said nothing. The signal of quality wasn't how much we pulled out. It was how much we were disciplined enough to cut.

That corpus is the moat. Not the model. Not the prompt. The captured judgment.

Everything else is rented.

## AI-Native Beats Bolted-On — Every Time

Companies built around AI from day one outrun the ones retrofitting it. Not sometimes. Every time.

When AI is the foundation, the product and the data model are designed around what the tech can actually do. Bolt it on later and you get a feature stapled to a legacy architecture. Slower iteration. Shallower moats.

The proof is in our own build: the governance layer — the versioned rules for how we judge — isn't a setting buried in an admin panel. It's the spine. A competitor can call the exact same API we do. They can't call our constitution.

And here's what surprised me: you can see the difference within minutes of a demo. The AI-native product breathes. The bolted-on one strains — you feel the seams where the old system refused to bend. That gap isn't closing. It's widening.

## The Calibration Gap Is the Work

Point the best frontier model at a stack of decks and ask it to score them like an investor would. It won't.

Out of the box, our system and our partners barely agreed. And when they diverged, the machine was almost always the optimist in the room — generous where a partner would've been brutal.

That gap is not a footnote. That gap _is_ the product.

Most teams see early divergence, call it "directional," and ship. We measured it, stared at it, and closed it — slowly, through relentless calibration against real human reviews. The model gets you to the starting line. The loop is the race.

Clean data is the same fight wearing different clothes. One bad input and the system hallucinates — confidently. It doesn't fail loudly. It fails fluently. Nobody claps for a deduplicated corpus, which is exactly why most teams underinvest in the one thing separating a tool you trust from a liability that's wrong with a straight face.

## Be Honest About What the Machine Actually Does

The AI scores the macro fundamentals well — high-level team analysis, problem, solution, stated market, financials. Human reviewers go deeper. They push past the stated views and ask the second-, third-, and fourth-level questions. Is that market size real, or backed into from a number the founder wanted to hit? Does the traction actually attribute to the product, or to a coincidence of timing? The judgment-heavy work still sits with people.

We didn't hide that line. We designed around it — and we keep moving it. As the calibration tightens, the AI takes on more. Today's limit is a floor, not a ceiling.

The temptation in this wave is to claim the machine does everything. The discipline is naming what it doesn't — yet. Stating the limit out loud is the only thing that makes the part it _does_ do believable. Overclaim once and an insider stops trusting all of it.

## Where the Value Actually Hides

Here's the lesson under all the other lessons.

The model labs are cleaning up everything deterministic, easy, and out in the open. Anything with one right answer — extract this field, summarize this doc, write this function — is getting absorbed into the frontier models themselves, for free, on their roadmap. If your startup lives there, you're a feature waiting to be deprecated.

So where does durable value go? It flees to three places the labs can't easily follow.

The non-deterministic. Evaluating a founder has no single right answer. Neither does scoring a deck, sizing a market, or calling a turnaround. It's judgment — contested, contextual, deeply human. The labs can't ship how a seasoned investor reads a founder — the instinct that separates a fundable team from a well-rehearsed one — because that lives in human heads, not in a benchmark.

The hard-to-reach data. A frontier model has read the entire public internet. It hasn't read what nobody publishes — the proprietary, the permissioned, the locked-up, the data you assemble one relationship and one painful integration at a time. The harder a dataset is to reach, the less likely a lab ever touches it. We didn't pull our 25,000 decks off a shelf. There is no shelf.

The genuinely hard problems. Not fiddly — hard. Years of domain pain, ugly edge cases, workflows no outsider understands. The labs optimize for the general case. The brutal, narrow, unglamorous problems are exactly the ones they skip — and exactly where a focused team builds something that lasts.

The through-line: the fuzziness, the inaccessibility, the sheer difficulty — everything the market treats as a bug — is the moat. The harder a thing is to make deterministic, to reach, or to solve, the longer it stays yours.

That's the counterintuitive part. In a world racing toward deterministic perfection, the defensible companies are the ones working exactly where it's hardest to follow.

## Why We Put Ourselves Through This

The goal never changes. How do we create alpha for our LPs?

In this wave, you can't answer that from the cheap seats. The memos are downstream of someone

6dd0ac01a715d809.subject: How to use GPT-5.6
6dd0ac01a715d809.body:
View this post on the web at https://www.bensbites.com/p/how-to-use-gpt-56

Hey folks,
OpenAI finally released the GPT-5.6 models to everyone [ https://substack.com/redirect/452db518-6045-49df-8c91-12f59ed4837c?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ], with many product changes. ChatGPT’s macOS app and the Codex app are now merged, with a new mode called ChatGPT Work [ https://substack.com/redirect/53172bf2-795b-45b1-9419-5b29f62940c3?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]. You can update your Codex app and get the new combined app. Codex and Work look similar, with the details fine-tuned for coding-related vs non-coding-related work. There’s a new plugin called “ChatGPT Sites” that lets you build hosted websites with an optional “Login with ChatGPT” feature. It’s nice for sharing  but kept annoying me by making everything that I ask on ChatGPT Sites, so I turned it off.
GPT-5.6 series has three models: Luna, Terra and Sol. Each of them comes with 5 thinking levels - light, medium, high, xhigh, max, and a new mode: Ultra mode. Ultra mode basically allows these models to go haywire with subagents. 
Now… these models eat up your usage limits much faster when used at higher thinking levels. So don’t rip ultra; first time I’ve ever been nearly out of usage in Codex. I’m defaulting to sol medium for most building/creativity, background agents for harder tasks and day-to-day productivity using luna xhigh.
Some general patterns I’ve noticed:
Sol is pretty good at UI, but it’s even better once you give it some references. Sol at Max thinking has really good writing, and it’s fun to chat with.
Terra feels like a replacement for 5.5 with minor improvements in UI and writing skills. It also feels more steerable, so skills would be useful with it. 
Luna has a bit of a mini model smell, like sometimes it doesn’t “get” what you meant in ambiguous prompts. But it doesn’t fail at tasks you clearly define.
Over the weekend, OpenAI reset usage 4-5 times while they were fixing bugs introduced during the app merge, and they have temporarily removed the 5h limit on your usage. Take note of that because you might end up using up your weekly limits in one go.
These models in the Codex app are really good at Computer Use, i.e., self-driving your cursor so it can open apps, click buttons, and use them by looking at the screen. You should definitely try this with Sol medium/high for some small task to see it in action.
Further reading:
Sol helped post-train Luna [ https://substack.com/redirect/b9df0b67-b84d-41ac-b401-a618f3f9fd3f?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ].
Train a model on your iMessages [ https://substack.com/redirect/de0eea90-86fe-4b5c-888f-53ce2a0ed622?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ].
Using GPT-5.6 without burning limits [ https://substack.com/redirect/45798994-dd63-4eb0-adf2-111ccac1ee8d?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ].
Ben’s Bites is brought to you by Voices [ https://substack.com/redirect/4a23477f-1732-4c32-ab09-dc7563e8d8e1?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ]
Building or training voice models? Stop scraping. Voices [ https://substack.com/redirect/4a23477f-1732-4c32-ab09-dc7563e8d8e1?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] has professionally directed, fully consented voice data ready now—part of 100,000+ hours of custom, premium data. Character performances, 43 emotional states, 8 languages. Request a sample today [ https://substack.com/redirect/4a23477f-1732-4c32-ab09-dc7563e8d8e1?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ].
Headlines
Anthropic again extended Fable 5 [ https://substack.com/redirect/9a9e5c03-3d89-4756-86e6-1a902180cfa7?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] on paid plans and Claude Code’s 50% higher weekly limits through July 19. This feels like a joke at this point.
Claude Code got an in-app browser [ https://substack.com/redirect/cf35704e-1369-48df-b95e-a86287489b01?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - let Claude open and click through docs, designs or your app. Claude Code Artifacts [ https://substack.com/redirect/b30f6f56-4728-4173-b783-289e910a0eb3?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] now support public links and multiplayer editing, including pages made through Claude Tag in Slack.
Meta released Muse Spark 1.1 [ https://substack.com/redirect/f5c79775-634e-4af6-99c4-95717d463c65?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - a multimodal model for coding, computer use and agent tasks with a 1M token context window. Meta is also starting to offer its models in the API now, and Muse Spark 1.1 is available at a relatively cheaper price point vs its peers.
Apple is suing OpenAI [ https://substack.com/redirect/dac96ce3-d348-46ef-a8e0-04fea5a8c5c8?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] and two former employees over alleged trade-secret theft for its upcoming AI hardware. Apple says candidates were told to share confidential information and bring physical parts to interviews; OpenAI says it has no interest in others’ secrets and is reviewing the case.
Notion Ship OS [ https://substack.com/redirect/f67776ea-0e04-4ad6-8c10-a84ae54429d8?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] runs product development from customer feedback to a merged PR in one workspace. Agents triage, route, and summarise work while people make the judgment calls, using Notion’s existing docs and databases.
My feed
The future worth building is human [ https://substack.com/redirect/ac48624f-187d-4025-b508-a29de757a7dd?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - New mission statement from Thinking Machines making the case for many AIs shaped by local knowledge.
Tend [ https://substack.com/redirect/1868244f-dcdf-4d03-b0a4-2e799d1a1598?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - open-source ChatGPT Work loop for inboxes, hiring or support that learns from your approvals.
v0 Design Systems 2.0 [ https://substack.com/redirect/c1d06f38-8a74-4668-aef9-6a92db1a3fac?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - import components, Figma and Storybook so generated apps use your real system.
NameThat [ https://substack.com/redirect/242ae1c0-5c11-4c50-9569-2fb0b752c050?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - visual dictionary for UI patterns you can see but do not know how to describe to an agent.
OptionAFK [ https://substack.com/redirect/f9350ba1-1049-48c0-b1f8-3c6075a4c8c6?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - Private and fast dictation + transcription toolkit that your agents can use.
shadcn/typeset [ https://substack.com/redirect/0de650b2-6bea-4320-940d-82194def16ab?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - one editable CSS file for styling Markdown in blogs, docs and streaming chat.
Inference AutoTune [ https://substack.com/redirect/932fa789-11bf-419f-8d59-2c729e6d8b79?j=eyJ1IjoiMmU2aDEifQ.VGKrlpe010xZBcMagL3trNwkDdJUfw_qmH45MbfoDHA ] - claims to distil a frontier model into your own 1-30B specialist in ~2 hours for under $250.

As promised, I am going to do a few posts in the coming days about the state of affairs in Russia.
So this is the first of two posts this week that focus on ideas recently expressed by senior figures in Russia. Together, they paint a revealing picture of the mindset that now dominates much of Moscow’s strategic thinking.
